title: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases
section: Genetics, Aging, and Frontiers in Medicine
authors: David M. Morens, Anthony S. Fauci

items.subtopic: Emerging and Re-Emerging Infectious Diseases | EMERGING INFECTIOUS DISEASES: DEFINITION AND CLASSIFICATION
items.title: Why Emerging Infectious Diseases (EIDs) Became More Prevalent in the Neolithic Era
items.content: The transition to the neolithic era marked a significant shift in human lifestyle, leading to increased domestication of animals, agricultural practices, and urbanization. These changes facilitated closer contact between humans and animals, creating opportunities for pathogens to spread more widely among human populations.
keyPoints: Pre-neolithic humans had limited contact with other humans or animals, restricting the spread of non-endemic pathogens.
The neolithic era began around 10,000 BCE, introducing domestication of animals, agriculture, and urbanization.
These societal changes increased human crowding and animal-human contacts, enabling the emergence and spread of significant EIDs.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · Emerging and Re-Emerging Infectious Diseases | EMERGING INFECTIOUS DISEASES: DEFINITION AND CLASSIFICATION

items.subtopic: Emerging and Re-Emerging Infectious Diseases | EMERGING INFECTIOUS DISEASES: DEFINITION AND CLASSIFICATION
items.title: How to Differentiate Between Emerging and Re-emerging Infectious Diseases (REIDs)
items.content: Clinicians must distinguish between newly emerging infectious diseases (EIDs) and re-emerging infectious diseases (REIDs). REIDs can include pathogens that were previously controlled but have resurfaced, sometimes due to human activities such as accidental release from research or vaccines.
keyPoints: EIDs are defined by their new appearance in a population, while REIDs involve the return of previously controlled infections.
Clinicians play a crucial role in identifying both EIDs and REIDs due to their expertise.
REIDs can result from accidental releases, such as vaccine-derived polioviruses or unexpected resurgences like the 1977 H1N1 influenza virus.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · Emerging and Re-Emerging Infectious Diseases | EMERGING INFECTIOUS DISEASES: DEFINITION AND CLASSIFICATION

items.title: Impact of Emerging Infectious Diseases (EIDs) on Human History
items.content: Emerging infectious diseases have historically been significant causes of death, disability, and social disruption. EIDs are newly recognized in humans or previously known but now appearing in new locations or forms.
keyPoints: EIDs include HIV/AIDS, Nipah virus infection, SARS, and COVID-19.
Historical examples: Plague of Athens (430-425 BCE), Justinian plague (544 AD), Black Death (1347–1349 AD).
COVID-19 pandemic has killed more than 7 million people as of 2024.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · EMERGING INFECTIOUS DISEASES: THEIR IMPORTANCE

items.title: Characteristics and Impact of Re-emerging Infectious Diseases (REIDs)
items.content: Re-emerging infectious diseases are those that have reappeared in new locations, resistant forms, or after control efforts. They can be due to accidental release or intentional harm.
keyPoints: Examples include West Nile virus, drug-resistant malaria, and poliomyelitis in parts of Africa.
Subcategories: vaccine-derived polioviruses, bioterrorism agents like the 2001 anthrax attacks.
Cholera re-emerged post-natural disasters, e.g., Haiti in 2010.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · EMERGING INFECTIOUS DISEASES: THEIR IMPORTANCE

items.subtopic: RESPONSE OF THE MEDICAL COMMUNITY TO INFECTIOUS DISEASES
items.title: Impact of Overconfidence in Infectious Disease Control
items.content: The early successes in treating infectious diseases led to overconfidence, with experts predicting the conquest of such diseases by the 1960s. This shift in focus was evident when U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention reorganized towards chronic and lifestyle-associated diseases.
keyPoints: By the 1960s, experts predicted that infectious diseases would be conquered.
U.S. CDC reorganization in 1981 shifted focus from IDs to chronic and lifestyle-associated diseases.
The emergence of AIDS in 1981 challenged this overconfidence.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · RESPONSE OF THE MEDICAL COMMUNITY TO INFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.subtopic: RESPONSE OF THE MEDICAL COMMUNITY TO INFECTIOUS DISEASES
items.title: Global Infrastructure for Emerging Infectious Disease Control
items.content: Modern global infrastructure includes vigorous detection and response to EIDs, with international health regulations overseen by WHO. Successes include stopping SARS from becoming a pandemic, turning AIDS into a manageable condition, and controlling the Ebola outbreak without vaccines.
keyPoints: SARS was stopped from becoming globally pandemic and eradicated as a human pathogen.
AIDS management improved with antiretroviral therapies, extending lifespan for patients.
The 2014-2016 Ebola outbreak in West Africa was controlled using standard public health measures alone.
Smallpox was declared eradicated in 1980; rinderpest in 2011.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · RESPONSE OF THE MEDICAL COMMUNITY TO INFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.title: Understanding Point-Source Exposures in Emerging Pathogens
items.content: Point-source exposures lead to sporadic, unpredictable outbreaks of high morbidity but low mortality. These infections arise from environmental sources and are typically short-lived.
keyPoints: Examples include coccidioidomycosis, histoplasmosis, norovirus on cruise ships, and foodborne bacterial/toxin contamination.
Outbreaks are characterized by high morbidity but low mortality.
Pathogens are usually well known and easily diagnosed or tested.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · MECHANISMS OF EMERGENCE

items.title: Animal-to-Human Host Switching in Pandemics
items.content: Host switching is a critical mechanism for both emerging and re-emerging diseases, often leading to pandemics. This process involves pathogens adapting from animals to humans.
keyPoints: Examples include the 1918 influenza pandemic, HIV/AIDS, human mpox, and Rift Valley fever.
Pathogens typically have high mutation rates, especially RNA viruses.
Emergence requires a combination of mutation, unusual human-animal contact, and chance.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · MECHANISMS OF EMERGENCE

items.title: Mutation and Adaptation in Human-Adapted Pathogens
items.content: Mutations in already human-adapted pathogens can lead to increased transmissibility, resistance, or pathogenicity. This is exemplified by antibiotic-resistant bacteria and viral mutations.
keyPoints: Antibiotic resistance in bacteria is a key example of mutation-driven emergence.
Viruses like SARS-CoV-2 delta variant may mutate for greater transmissibility or immune escape.
Enterovirus D68 re-emerged with increased pathogenicity, causing acute flaccid myelitis.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · MECHANISMS OF EMERGENCE

items.title: Human Activities as Catalysts for Pathogen Emergence
items.content: Pathogen emergence is largely driven by human activities and environmental changes. Key factors include demographics, behavior (such as crowding and sexual practices), and occupation, alongside environmental degradation due to poverty, wars, land use practices, and inadequate public health infrastructure.
keyPoints: Human activities such as urbanization, transnational travel, and commercial sex work facilitate pathogen transmission.
Environmental changes like deforestation and intensive farming can lead to new disease emergence (e.g., Nipah virus in Malaysia).
Societal failures and ecosystem disruptions contribute to re-emergence of known pathogens.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · VARIABLES ASSOCIATED WITH DISEASE EMERGENCE

items.title: Patterns and Predictability of Pathogen Emergence
items.content: While human activities significantly influence pathogen emergence, the intervals between pandemics for diseases like influenza remain consistent over centuries. This suggests that such events are rare stochastic occurrences rather than direct results of population size or density.
keyPoints: Intervals between influenza pandemics have remained about the same for 500 years despite global population growth and rapid travel.
Emergence of new pathogens can be unique and highly improbable, while re-emergence often results from societal failures.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · VARIABLES ASSOCIATED WITH DISEASE EMERGENCE

items.subtopic: EXAMPLES OF CHALLENGES OF DISEASE EMERGENCE AND HOW THEY ARE BEING MET | INFLUENZA A
items.title: Mechanisms of Influenza A Mutation and Immune Evasion
items.content: Influenza A viruses have multiple mechanisms to evade human immunity, including genetic drift, shift, intrasubtype reassortments, and glycosylation. These mutations allow the virus to escape population immunity from natural infection and vaccination.
keyPoints: Genetic 'drift' involves point mutations.
Genetic 'shift' includes importation of different avian hemagglutinin with or without neuraminidases.
Intrasubtype reassortments involve the importation of whole human hemagglutinin or neuraminidase subtype variants.
Glycosylation can alter viral structure and function.
The 1968 pandemic H3N2 virus has been causing infections and escaping vaccine immunity for over 56 years.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · EXAMPLES OF CHALLENGES OF DISEASE EMERGENCE AND HOW THEY ARE BEING MET | INFLUENZA A

items.subtopic: EXAMPLES OF CHALLENGES OF DISEASE EMERGENCE AND HOW THEY ARE BEING MET | INFLUENZA A
items.title: Challenges in Influenza Control Due to Viral Replication and Immune Evasion
items.content: Influenza A replicates on a one-cell-deep layer of respiratory epithelium, avoiding full exposure to the systemic immune system. Its short incubation period limits the elicitation of memory immune cells, relying instead on innate immunity and IgA/IgM antibodies.
keyPoints: Replicates in the upper respiratory tract, tolerating continuous antigenic exposures without harmful immune responses.
Short incubation period prevents full elicitation of memory immune cells.
Vaccines are incompletely effective and provide protection for only a few months.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · EXAMPLES OF CHALLENGES OF DISEASE EMERGENCE AND HOW THEY ARE BEING MET | INFLUENZA A

items.subtopic: EXAMPLES OF CHALLENGES OF DISEASE EMERGENCE AND HOW THEY ARE BEING MET | INFLUENZA A
items.title: Impact of Modern Transportation on Influenza Spread
items.content: Influenza spread has adapted to modern transportation methods, with pandemics increasingly spreading rapidly via air travel. High rates of presymptomatic and subsymptomatic infection facilitate transmission by unaware individuals.
keyPoints: 1889 pandemic spread from East Asia to Europe by rail.
1918 and 1957 pandemics spread predominantly by ship.
1968 and 2009 pandemics spread predominantly by air.
High rates of presymptomatic and subsymptomatic infection allow transmission by unaware individuals.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · EXAMPLES OF CHALLENGES OF DISEASE EMERGENCE AND HOW THEY ARE BEING MET | INFLUENZA A

items.title: ACE2 Receptor Role in Sarbecovirus Infection
items.content: Sarbecoviruses, including SARS-CoV2, utilize the ACE2 receptor for entry into human cells. This differs from influenza viruses which use respiratory tract sialic acid receptors.
keyPoints: Natural reservoir of sarbecoviruses is bats, not birds.
Main human host receptor for viral infection: angiotensin-converting enzyme 2 (ACE2).
Potential preadaptation to humans due to similar ACE2 receptors in bats and other mammals.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · SARS AND SARS-CoV2

items.title: Challenges in Controlling SARS-CoV2 Spread
items.content: Despite moderate transmissibility, SARS-CoV2 can cause superspreading events in crowded environments with poor airflow and humidity. It evolves by mutation and recombination, leading to challenges in achieving population immunity and vaccine prevention.
keyPoints: SARs-CoV2 is a respiratory virus without true viremia.
Short incubation period and often asymptomatic or subsymptomatic infection complicate control measures.
Can be superspread in crowded environments with poor airflow and humidity.
Evolves by mutation and recombination, escaping population immunity.
Vaccines provide imperfect prevention.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · SARS AND SARS-CoV2

items.title: Understanding Dengue Virus Serotypes and Immunity
items.content: Dengue virus consists of four serotypes (DEN-1, DEN-2, DEN-3, and DEN-4) that interact serologically. The development of dengue hemorrhagic fever (DHF) or dengue shock syndrome (DSS) is predominantly associated with a second infection by a different serotype due to antibody-dependent enhancement.
keyPoints: Four closely related dengue virus serotypes: DEN-1, DEN-2, DEN-3, and DEN-4.
Cross-reactive antibodies from the first infection can enhance viral entry during a subsequent infection with a different serotype, leading to DHF/DSS.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · DENGUE

items.title: Epidemiology and Control Challenges of Dengue
items.content: Despite advanced public health measures, dengue remains difficult to control due to the mosquito's ability to breed in minimal water sources. The disease is highly endemic around tropical regions and re-emerges sporadically causing significant morbidity.
keyPoints: Dengue has been endemic around the tropical belt for over 400 years.
Even with stringent public health measures, dengue outbreaks occur due to mosquitoes breeding in tiny water sources.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · DENGUE

items.title: Clinical Impact and Management of Severe Dengue
items.content: Severe forms of dengue, such as DHF and DSS, are characterized by sudden massive fluid extravasation. Recent advances include training for clinicians, oral rehydration solutions to manage shock syndrome, and the development of vaccines, though concerns about vaccine safety persist.
keyPoints: DHF/DSS involves severe fluid leakage into tissues.
Oral rehydration solutions are crucial in managing dengue shock syndrome.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · DENGUE

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF CLINICIANS AND LABORATORIANS IN EID CONTROL
items.title: Recognizing Emerging Infectious Diseases (EIDs) through Unusual Clinical Presentations
items.content: Clinicians are often the first to identify new diseases due to atypical signs, symptoms, or complications. Historical examples include the 1918 influenza pandemic with pneumonia-associated case-fatality and dengue hemorrhagic fever's unique age-specific mortality patterns.
keyPoints: Astute clinicians recognize EIDs by unusual clinical presentations.
1918 influenza featured viral infection with bacterial co-pathogenesis, leading to bronchopneumonic pneumonia.
Dengue hemorrhagic fever had novel epidemiologic patterns and complications like shock in specific age groups.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · THE ROLE OF CLINICIANS AND LABORATORIANS IN EID CONTROL

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF CLINICIANS AND LABORATORIANS IN EID CONTROL
items.title: The Importance of Interdisciplinary Collaboration in EID Identification and Control
items.content: Effective identification and control of EIDs require collaboration among clinicians, pathologists, epidemiologists, and public health workers. Historical examples include the identification of Pneumocystis carinii pneumonia by pathologists and the role of pediatric neurologists in recognizing enterovirus D68-associated acute flaccid myelitis.
keyPoints: Interdisciplinary collaboration is crucial for EID control.
Pathologists identified Pneumocystis carinii pneumonia, contributing to AIDS characterization.
Pediatric neurologists helped identify enterovirus D68-associated acute flaccid myelitis.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · THE ROLE OF CLINICIANS AND LABORATORIANS IN EID CONTROL

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF CLINICIANS AND LABORATORIANS IN EID CONTROL
items.title: Key Skills for Clinicians in Managing Emerging Infectious Diseases
items.content: Clinicians must possess curiosity, the ability to recognize unusual patterns, and strong relationships with laboratories and public health workers. These skills are essential for early recognition, diagnosis, and control of EIDs.
keyPoints: Curiosity and suspicion are vital for recognizing EIDs.
Understanding disease epidemiology aids in identifying acquisition mechanisms.
Strong laboratory and public health relationships enhance EID management.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · THE ROLE OF CLINICIANS AND LABORATORIANS IN EID CONTROL

items.question: Which period is associated with the emergence of significant emerging infectious diseases (EIDs) due to changes in human societal practices?
items.explanation: The neolithic age, around 10,000 BCE, saw domestication of animals and growth of villages, leading to the emergence of significant EIDs.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · Emerging and Re-Emerging Infectious Diseases | EMERGING INFECTIOUS DISEASES: DEFINITION AND CLASSIFICATION

items.question: Which factor is NOT mentioned as contributing to the spread of pathogens during the neolithic age?
items.explanation: The passage does not mention increased human mobility across continents as a factor during the neolithic age.

items.question: Which of the following is an example of a re-emerging infectious disease (REID) due to accidental release?
items.explanation: The passage mentions vaccine-derived polioviruses as an example of REIDs due to accidental release.

items.question: Which of the following is an example of a re-emerging infectious disease (REID) that has appeared in resistant forms?
items.explanation: Methicillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus is a re-emerging infectious disease that has appeared in resistant forms.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · EMERGING INFECTIOUS DISEASES: THEIR IMPORTANCE

items.question: Which pandemic was thought to be the deadliest in human history?
items.explanation: The 1918 H1N1 influenza pandemic is thought to have been the deadliest pandemic in human history.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · EMERGING INFECTIOUS DISEASES: THEIR IMPORTANCE

items.question: Which of the following pandemics occurred during the past century and has killed more than 40 million people?
items.explanation: The HIV/AIDS pandemic has killed more than 40 million people.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · EMERGING INFECTIOUS DISEASES: THEIR IMPORTANCE

items.subtopic: RESPONSE OF THE MEDICAL COMMUNITY TO INFECTIOUS DISEASES
items.question: Which of the following was a significant factor in the U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention's reorganization in 1981?
options: The prediction that infectious diseases would be conquered.
The emergence of AIDS caused by HIV.
The development of antivirals by the 1950s.
items.explanation: The reorganization was influenced by the unexpected emergence of the AIDS pandemic in 1981.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · RESPONSE OF THE MEDICAL COMMUNITY TO INFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.subtopic: RESPONSE OF THE MEDICAL COMMUNITY TO INFECTIOUS DISEASES
items.question: What was one major outcome of the biomedical research community's response to the AIDS pandemic?
options: Development of effective combination antiretroviral therapies.
Eradication of HIV as a human pathogen.
Prediction that infectious diseases would be conquered.
items.explanation: The response included the development of effective combination antiretroviral therapies for AIDS.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · RESPONSE OF THE MEDICAL COMMUNITY TO INFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.subtopic: RESPONSE OF THE MEDICAL COMMUNITY TO INFECTIOUS DISEASES
items.question: Which disease was declared eradicated in 2011?
items.explanation: Rinderpest, a veterinary disease, was declared eradicated in 2011.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · RESPONSE OF THE MEDICAL COMMUNITY TO INFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.question: Which type of pathogen emergence is characterized by infections that are not usually transmitted onward to other humans and thus do not become epidemic?
items.explanation: Dead-end infections, such as those from point-source exposures, are not usually transmitted onward and do not become epidemic.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · MECHANISMS OF EMERGENCE

items.question: What is a common example of an emerging pathogen that results from animal-to-human host switching?
items.explanation: HIV/AIDS is an example of a disease that emerged through animal-to-human host switching.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · MECHANISMS OF EMERGENCE

items.question: What factor contributes to the emergence of pathogens into new host species according to current theories?
options: High mutation rates and chance events
items.explanation: Pathogen emergence into new host species is theorized to result from high mutation rates, intense or unusual human-animal contact, and chance.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · MECHANISMS OF EMERGENCE

items.question: What phenomenon can lead to increased pathogenicity in viruses as they adapt to larger human populations?
options: Mutation leading to a more transmissible phenotype
items.explanation: Mutations may lead to a fitness advantage, such as increased transmissibility and pathogenicity in viruses.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · MECHANISMS OF EMERGENCE

items.question: Which of the following factors is most likely to contribute to the emergence of a new infectious disease?
options: Stable ecosystems with minimal human interference
Decreased population density in rural areas
items.explanation: Emergence of new infectious diseases is often associated with increased global travel, urbanization, and human activities that disturb ecosystems.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · VARIABLES ASSOCIATED WITH DISEASE EMERGENCE

items.question: What was a contributing factor to the emergence of hantavirus pulmonary syndrome?
options: Deforestation leading to increased rodent populations
Urban crowding and domestic water storage
items.explanation: Hantavirus pulmonary syndrome emerged due to the construction of human-made structures that housed infected rodents.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · VARIABLES ASSOCIATED WITH DISEASE EMERGENCE

items.question: Which factor was associated with the emergence of Nipah virus in Malaysia?
options: Urban crowding and domestic water storage
Deforestation and intensive pig farming practices
items.explanation: The emergence of Nipah virus in Malaysia was linked to deforestation and intensive pig farming.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · VARIABLES ASSOCIATED WITH DISEASE EMERGENCE

items.question: Which of the following mechanisms allows influenza A virus to escape human immunity?
items.explanation: Influenza A escapes human immunity through genetic drift, which involves point mutations.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · EXAMPLES OF CHALLENGES OF DISEASE EMERGENCE AND HOW THEY ARE BEING MET | INFLUENZA A

items.question: What is the primary reservoir for influenza A viruses?
items.explanation: The primary reservoir for influenza A viruses is wild waterfowl and shorebirds.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · EXAMPLES OF CHALLENGES OF DISEASE EMERGENCE AND HOW THEY ARE BEING MET | INFLUENZA A

items.subtopic: EXAMPLES OF CHALLENGES OF DISEASE EMERGENCE AND HOW THEY ARE BEING MET | INFLUENZA A
items.question: Why are influenza vaccines only partially effective?
options: Influenza replicates in the systemic immune system.
The virus has a long incubation period allowing full immune response.
Vaccines do not encounter the specific innate immune system.
They protect for only a matter of months due to viral mutations.
items.explanation: Influenza vaccines are partially effective because they protect for only a short period due to viral mutations.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · EXAMPLES OF CHALLENGES OF DISEASE EMERGENCE AND HOW THEY ARE BEING MET | INFLUENZA A

items.subtopic: EXAMPLES OF CHALLENGES OF DISEASE EMERGENCE AND HOW THEY ARE BEING MET | INFLUENZA A
items.question: How has the spread of influenza pandemics changed over time?
options: Spread by coach in modern times.
Increased speed due to air travel.
Reduced spread due to better hygiene.
items.explanation: The spread of influenza pandemics has increased in speed due to advancements like air travel.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · EXAMPLES OF CHALLENGES OF DISEASE EMERGENCE AND HOW THEY ARE BEING MET | INFLUENZA A

items.question: What is the natural reservoir for sarbecoviruses?
items.explanation: The natural reservoir for sarbecoviruses is bats, not birds.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · SARS AND SARS-CoV2

items.question: Which receptor do SARS-like beta-coronaviruses primarily use to infect human cells?
items.explanation: SARS-like beta-coronaviruses primarily use the ACE2 receptor to infect human cells.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · SARS AND SARS-CoV2

items.question: How do SARS-CoV2 viruses evolve and escape population immunity?
items.explanation: SARS-CoV2 evolves and escapes population immunity by mutation and recombination, not by genetic reassortment.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · SARS AND SARS-CoV2

items.question: Which of the following is NOT a factor associated with the re-emergence of dengue?
items.explanation: Dengue is transmitted via a human-to-mosquito-to-human cycle, not directly from human to human.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · DENGUE

items.question: What was the major epidemic location for dengue in the United States before it became more localized?
items.explanation: A major epidemic occurred in Philadelphia in 1780.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · DENGUE

items.question: Which of the following statements about dengue virus serotypes is correct?
options: There are two closely related viruses.
DEN-1 and DEN-2 cause DHF/DSS only.
All four serotypes can potentially cause severe forms like DHF/DSS.
DEN-3 and DEN-4 do not interact with each other.
items.explanation: All four dengue viruses (DEN-1, DEN-2, DEN-3, DEN-4) can cause severe forms like DHF/DSS, particularly on a second infection.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · DENGUE

items.question: What is the primary reason Singapore struggles to prevent dengue outbreaks despite excellent sanitation?
options: Lack of public health mosquito police
Mosquitoes can oviposit in very small water bodies.
Absence of air conditioning in residences
items.explanation: Mosquitoes can lay eggs in tiny and inconspicuous bits of water, making it difficult to control.

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF CLINICIANS AND LABORATORIANS IN EID CONTROL
items.question: What was the key observation that led to the identification of legionellosis as an emerging disease?
options: Clinicians were unable to make a pathogen-specific pneumonia diagnosis.
Laboratories isolated a new strain of bacteria.
Patients exhibited typical viral respiratory symptoms.
There was a sudden increase in antibiotic resistance.
items.explanation: Legionellosis was identified as an emerging disease when clinicians were unable to make a pathogen-specific pneumonia diagnosis and laboratories were unable to isolate a pathogen.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · THE ROLE OF CLINICIANS AND LABORATORIANS IN EID CONTROL

Close relationship with public health workers
items.explanation: The passage does not mention knowledge of advanced surgical techniques as a skill related to dealing with EIDs.

options: They are responsible for vaccine development.
They recognize new diseases through atypical signs and symptoms.
They primarily focus on administrative tasks.
items.explanation: Clinicians play a role in recognizing new diseases, often because the signs, symptoms, or complications are not typical of similar diseases.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · THE ROLE OF CLINICIANS AND LABORATORIANS IN EID CONTROL

items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · RESPONSE OF THE MEDICAL COMMUNITY TO INFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.subtopic: RESPONSE OF THE MEDICAL COMMUNITY TO INFECTIOUS DISEASES
items.statement: The 1992 report by the Institute of Medicine highlighted the problem of emerging infections and made recommendations for preparedness.
items.explanation: The IOM report drew attention to EIDs, coined the term, and recommended measures for preparedness.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · RESPONSE OF THE MEDICAL COMMUNITY TO INFECTIOUS DISEASES

items.statement: Most animal and human pathogens are adapted to a wide range of host species.
items.explanation: Most animal and human pathogens, especially viruses infecting mammals, are specifically adapted to a narrow host group.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · MECHANISMS OF EMERGENCE

items.statement: Antibiotic-resistant pathogens have only emerged since the development of antibiotics.
items.explanation: Antibiotic-resistant pathogens exist in natural environments and have done so before human-developed antibiotics.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · MECHANISMS OF EMERGENCE

items.statement: Re-emergences of known pathogens are more likely due to unique and highly improbable events.
items.explanation: Re-emergences are often due to societal failures and ecosystem disruptions, not unique events.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · VARIABLES ASSOCIATED WITH DISEASE EMERGENCE

items.statement: The intervals between influenza pandemics have shortened with increased global travel.
items.explanation: Intervals between influenza pandemics remain about the same despite increased global travel.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · VARIABLES ASSOCIATED WITH DISEASE EMERGENCE

items.subtopic: EXAMPLES OF CHALLENGES OF DISEASE EMERGENCE AND HOW THEY ARE BEING MET | INFLUENZA A
items.statement: Influenza A viruses replicate in a deep layer of respiratory epithelium.
items.explanation: Influenza A replicates on a one-cell-deep layer of respiratory epithelium.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · EXAMPLES OF CHALLENGES OF DISEASE EMERGENCE AND HOW THEY ARE BEING MET | INFLUENZA A

items.subtopic: EXAMPLES OF CHALLENGES OF DISEASE EMERGENCE AND HOW THEY ARE BEING MET | INFLUENZA A
items.statement: High rates of presymptomatic and subsymptomatic infection contribute to influenza transmission.
items.explanation: Presymptomatic and subsymptomatic infections allow the virus to be transmitted by unaware individuals.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · EXAMPLES OF CHALLENGES OF DISEASE EMERGENCE AND HOW THEY ARE BEING MET | INFLUENZA A

items.explanation: SARS-CoV2 does not produce true viremia.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · SARS AND SARS-CoV2

items.statement: Control of emerging SARS-CoV2 is difficult due to its moderate transmissibility and ability to be superspread in crowded environments.
items.explanation: The control of SARS-CoV2 remains difficult partly because it can be superspread in crowded environments.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · SARS AND SARS-CoV2

items.statement: Dengue virus has only one serotype.
items.explanation: There are four closely related dengue viruses (DEN-1, DEN-2, DEN-3, and DEN-4).
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · DENGUE

items.statement: Dengue hemorrhagic fever can occur with the first infection of any serotype.
items.explanation: DHF/DSS predominantly occurs on a second infection, often due to antibody-dependent enhancement.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · DENGUE

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF CLINICIANS AND LABORATORIANS IN EID CONTROL
items.statement: Ophthalmologists identified human Rift Valley fever in 1977 by observing unexpected blindness associated with macular infiltrates.
items.explanation: Emerging human Rift Valley fever was largely identified by ophthalmologists who observed severe and explosively epidemic conjunctivitis with blindness.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · THE ROLE OF CLINICIANS AND LABORATORIANS IN EID CONTROL

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF CLINICIANS AND LABORATORIANS IN EID CONTROL
items.statement: Pathologists were the first to identify AIDS in 1981 through autopsies of previously healthy young men.
items.explanation: In 1981, pathologists identified Pneumocystis carinii pneumonia in autopsies, contributing to characterizing a new clinical-pathologic syndrome recognized as AIDS.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · THE ROLE OF CLINICIANS AND LABORATORIANS IN EID CONTROL

items.assertion: Point-source exposures tend to be sporadic, unpredictable, and of very short duration.
items.reason: The causative organisms are from the environment and usually well known and easily diagnosed or tested for.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why point-source exposures have these characteristics.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · MECHANISMS OF EMERGENCE

items.assertion: Major pathogen emergences into new host species occur frequently.
items.reason: Pathogens constantly mutate, leading to frequent successful adaptations to new hosts.
items.explanation: The assertion is false because major emergencies are relatively uncommon despite constant mutations.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · MECHANISMS OF EMERGENCE

items.assertion: The emergence of new pathogens is often linked to human activities and environmental changes.
items.reason: Human movement, urbanization, and ecological disturbances create opportunities for pathogen emergence.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · VARIABLES ASSOCIATED WITH DISEASE EMERGENCE

items.subtopic: EXAMPLES OF CHALLENGES OF DISEASE EMERGENCE AND HOW THEY ARE BEING MET | INFLUENZA A
items.assertion: Influenza vaccines are at best incompletely effective.
items.reason: The virus replicates in an immune environment that tolerates multiple antigenic exposures without massive responses.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, but the reason is not the correct explanation for the vaccine's incomplete effectiveness.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · EXAMPLES OF CHALLENGES OF DISEASE EMERGENCE AND HOW THEY ARE BEING MET | INFLUENZA A

items.subtopic: EXAMPLES OF CHALLENGES OF DISEASE EMERGENCE AND HOW THEY ARE BEING MET | INFLUENZA A
items.assertion: The 1918 pandemic influenza H1N1 virus was derived directly from a waterfowl.
items.reason: Humans transmitted it to domestic pigs in 1918, which led to the current lineages.
items.explanation: The assertion is true; however, the reason is false as the virus may have had a brief period in another mammalian host before becoming pandemic.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · EXAMPLES OF CHALLENGES OF DISEASE EMERGENCE AND HOW THEY ARE BEING MET | INFLUENZA A

items.assertion: Sarbecoviruses are potentially preadapted to humans.
items.reason: ACE2 receptors on bats, many other mammals, and humans are quite similar.
items.explanation: The similarity of ACE2 receptors across species suggests sarbecoviruses could be preadapted to infect humans.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · SARS AND SARS-CoV2

items.assertion: The emergence of dengue hemorrhagic fever was linked to the interaction between different serotypes.
items.reason: Antibodies from a first infection with one serotype can enhance the severity of a second infection with another serotype.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · DENGUE

items.assertion: Singapore has completely eradicated dengue due to its advanced public health measures.
items.reason: Singapore employs mosquito police who patrol for breeding sites and enforce fines.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; despite these measures, Singapore still experiences dengue outbreaks.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · DENGUE

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF CLINICIANS AND LABORATORIANS IN EID CONTROL
items.assertion: Clinicians must have abiding curiosity to effectively deal with emerging infectious diseases.
items.reason: Abiding curiosity allows clinicians to sense something out of the ordinary and pursue suspicions using medical history, physical examination, and laboratory tests.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the reason correctly explains why abiding curiosity is important for clinicians in dealing with EIDs.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · THE ROLE OF CLINICIANS AND LABORATORIANS IN EID CONTROL

items.subtopic: THE ROLE OF CLINICIANS AND LABORATORIANS IN EID CONTROL
items.assertion: The identification of cervical cancer as a contagious disease was first proposed by an epidemiologist.
items.reason: A proto-epidemiologist demonstrated that nuns never suffered from cervical cancer, suggesting it might be contagious.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; it was a proto-epidemiologist, not an epidemiologist, who first proposed the idea. The reason is true.
items.reference: Emerging and Re Emerging Infectious Diseases · THE ROLE OF CLINICIANS AND LABORATORIANS IN EID CONTROL
